skill.name: red-team-tools
skill.description: Guides authorized red team and bug bounty workflows for reconnaissance, asset discovery, web testing, XSS hunting, and vulnerability triage.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized red team reconnaissance and web security testing workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external command and network findings are confirmed as documented red team command examples, not hidden execution by the skill itself. The skill is dual-use and includes active reconnaissance, fuzzing, XSS testing, and vulnerability scanning workflows, so it should publish only with clear authorization warnings. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential theft, persistence, malware behavior, or weak cryptographic implementation in the cited lines.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Active Reconnaissance Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run subdomain enumeration, live host discovery, directory fuzzing, API enumeration, and vulnerability scanning tools against target domains. This is legitimate for authorized security testing, but it can enable unauthorized reconnaissance if used outside scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections contain concrete commands for enumeration, fuzzing, API probing, and scanner execution. The context also includes scope and rate-limit constraints, so the finding is dual-use rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Automated XSS Testing Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes automated parameter extraction, payload replacement, reflection checks, and XSS scanner pipelines. These workflows are normal for authorized bug bounty testing, but they can be used to probe applications without permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directly shows XSS hunting pipelines and payload examples. It does not include exploit delivery beyond testing and verification, which keeps the risk at medium.

medium_findings.title: Reusable Shell Reconnaissance Script
medium_findings.description: The skill provides a shell script template that chains subdomain discovery, live host detection, URL collection, and Nuclei scanning for a supplied domain. The script is useful for authorized testing but increases operational risk because it automates network activity.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script content is explicit and includes multiple network-facing tools. Inputs are mostly quoted and no destructive payload is shown, so the concern is automated dual-use scanning rather than command injection.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Example Targets
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alerts point to public lookup or placeholder target.com examples used in documentation. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or exfiltrated data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in example commands and use placeholder domains. There is no code path that sends credentials or local files to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Alerts Not Substantiated
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts at the cited lines do not correspond to cryptographic code or algorithm selection. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptography usage in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of both cited lines shows metadata description text and generic usage text, not cryptographic operations. This is a high-confidence false positive.

low_findings.title: Scope and Rate-Limit Constraints Reduce Abuse Risk
low_findings.description: The skill explicitly tells users to respect program scope, avoid DoS or production fuzzing without permission, and rate limit requests. These statements do not remove dual-use risk, but they support an authorized testing intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The constraints are explicit and aligned with responsible security testing. They are guidance only, so they reduce but do not eliminate the operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Security Tool Invocation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents shell commands for Amass, Subfinder, httpx, httprobe, ffuf, Nuclei, Dalfox, and similar tools. These are true static positives for external command guidance, but they are documentation examples rather than concealed skill runtime execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and repeated across the methodology. The file is Markdown guidance, so the risk depends on user execution and authorization.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Reconnaissance and Vulnerability Scanner Pipelines
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented pipelines collect domains, probe live hosts, discover URLs, fuzz endpoints, and run vulnerability templates. These patterns can create noisy network traffic and must be limited to authorized scopes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands perform live network discovery, fuzzing, and vulnerability checks. The document repeatedly frames them as bug bounty or red team steps, not stealthy abuse.

content.value_statement: Security testers need repeatable workflows that keep reconnaissance organized and within approved scope. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structure authorized red team and bug bounty testing steps.
actual_capabilities: Organizes red team projects into reconnaissance, vulnerability, and reporting folders.
Guides subdomain enumeration with Amass, Subfinder, Assetfinder, Findomain, and dnsgen.
Plans live host discovery and technology fingerprinting with httprobe, httpx, WhatWeb, and Nuclei.
Outlines content discovery, API enumeration, and historical URL collection workflows.
Provides XSS hunting, parameter discovery, and vulnerability triage checklists.
Includes a simple recon script template for authorized target domains.
limitations: Requires explicit authorization and a defined testing scope before use.
Depends on external tools, wordlists, and API keys that are not included.
May produce false positives that require manual validation.
Can create network traffic that must be rate limited and monitored.

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Recon Planning
use_cases.description: Build a scoped reconnaissance checklist for a new bug bounty program.

use_cases.title: Internal Web Assessment
use_cases.description: Create an authorized workflow for mapping internal web assets and common exposure points.

use_cases.title: Finding Triage Preparation
use_cases.description: Convert scan outputs and manual observations into a structured validation plan.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Scoped Recon Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for an authorized target.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reconnaissance checklist for an authorized target domain. Include scope boundaries, required tools, output files, and manual validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Subdomain Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a subdomain enumeration workflow for my approved program scope. Separate passive discovery, active validation, deduplication, and live host checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design an XSS Testing Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an authorized XSS testing workflow for collected URLs. Include parameter discovery, payload safety, rate limits, and evidence needed for reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Triage Recon Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me triage reconnaissance results from an approved assessment. Prioritize hosts, technologies, endpoints, and vulnerabilities for manual verification.

output_examples.input: Create a recon plan for an authorized bug bounty target.
output: Scope summary with allowed domains and excluded systems.
Passive discovery steps before any active probing.
Live host validation plan with rate limits.
Manual review checklist for high-priority applications.

output_examples.input: Help me prepare an XSS hunting workflow.
output: Parameter collection steps from historical and live URLs.
Safe reflection checks before scanner use.
Verification requirements for stored and reflected issues.
Reporting notes for impact, evidence, and reproduction.

output_examples.input: Prioritize findings from a Nuclei scan.
output: Group results by severity, exposed asset, and confidence.
Identify likely false positives for manual review.
Recommend next validation steps within the approved scope.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization and scope before running any network command.
Rate limit scans and avoid production fuzzing unless the program explicitly allows it.
Manually validate every automated finding before reporting it.
anti_patterns: Using the workflow against targets without permission.
Running high-volume fuzzing without rate limits or monitoring.
Submitting raw scanner output without verification and impact analysis.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is dual-use and should be published with warnings. It is appropriate for authorized security testing only.

faq.question: Does the skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The file provides Markdown guidance and command examples that a user may choose to run.

faq.question: What tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references Amass, Subfinder, httpx, httprobe, ffuf, Nuclei, Dalfox, waybackurls, gau, and related security tools.

faq.question: Can it find real vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It can help plan testing workflows, but results depend on tools, scope, configuration, and manual validation.

faq.question: What permissions are required?
faq.answer: Users need explicit authorization for each target, including allowed domains, testing windows, and prohibited techniques.

faq.question: Does it include exploit code?
faq.answer: It includes XSS testing examples and scanner workflows, but no persistence, malware, credential theft, or destructive payloads were found.
